vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on cron.hourly
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/cron.hourly, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0700 /etc/cron.hourly
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then find -H /etc/cron.hourly/ -maxdepth 0 -perm /u+s,g+xwrs,o+xwrt -type d -exec chmod u-s,g-xwrs,o-xwrt {} \; else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The file /etc/cron.hourly should have permissions set to 0700 to restrict access to root only. Current permissions may be overly permissive, allowing unintended privilege escalation.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; Insecure file permissions; Privilege escalation

previous_attempts.scan_output: Single-rule scan failed for file_permissions_cron_hourly (rule=xccdf_org.ssgproject.content_rule_file_permissions_cron_hourly). Could not verify.

reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We are in the Remediation step. We must first review the plan, then apply the fix.

  The plan description: we are to run the command: chmod 0700 /etc/cron.hourly

  So we will call run_cmd with the command: chmod 0700 /etc/cron.hourly

  We must also check the file before? Actually, the rule says to set the permissions. We can do that by running chmod.0700. We don't need to read the file first? The rule does not require reading the file, but we can if we want. However, the execution rules say: ALWAYS use read_file to inspect the target config file BEFORE modifying it. So we must read the file.

  We will call read_file with path: /etc/cron.hourly  Then we will call run_cmd with command: chmod 0700 /etc/cron.hourly  We are done  Let's build the XML  We must output the function calls in the order: read_file, then run_cmd  We'll use the function tags with parameters  Note: the function call format: <function=...></function>  We must not have any extra text  Let's build:  First, read_file: <function=read_file> <parameter=path> /etc/cron.hourly </parameter> </function>  Then, run_cmd: <function=run_cmd> <parameter=command> chmod 0700 /etc/cron.hourly </parameter> </function>  Now, build the XML string:  Remember: the entire response must be a single string that is the XML  We output:  <function=read_file><parameter=path>/etc/cron.hourly</parameter></function><function=run_cmd><parameter=command>chmod 0700 /etc/cron.hourly</parameter></function>  We are done  Let's output that string.
